title: SEGMENT 14 — The seizure results — parent diary + EEG, the clearest signal

lines.text: SEGMENT 14 — The seizure results — parent diary + EEG, the clearest signal
The most clinically clear-cut result reported in the paper concerns seizures.
So let's spend a segment on that, because it is the place where the data are cleanest and the signal is strongest.
Before treatment, Mila was having approximately thirty seizures per day.
Documented by two independent measurement methods.
The first was a parent diary — her family kept careful daily records of every seizure, its time, its duration, its character.

The second was periodic EEG monitoring — electroencephalography, in which electrodes are placed on the scalp to record the electrical activity of the brain, allowing seizures to be detected and characterized electrographically even if their behavioral manifestations are subtle.
Both methods agreed.
Mila was having a lot of seizures.
The pre-treatment baseline daily count averaged thirty-one point five per day.
The average duration was about one hundred and eight seconds per seizure — nearly two minutes each.
And across the day, when you summed up the total time she spent in seizure activity, it averaged about three point nine percent of her waking time.
That's a substantial seizure burden — both quantitatively and qualitatively.

Each one of those seizures was a discrete neurological event, and thirty of them per day means her brain was repeatedly being interrupted by abnormal electrical activity throughout every day of her life.
After milasen treatment began, and as the loading phase progressed, the seizure numbers came down.
The reductions reported in the paper, comparing the pre-treatment baseline to the post-treatment period:
The daily seizure count fell by approximately sixty-three percent, from thirty-one point five per day to eleven point seven per day.
Average seizure duration fell by approximately fifty-two percent, from one hundred and eight seconds to fifty-two seconds.
And the percentage of time spent in seizure activity fell by approximately eighty-five percent, from three point nine percent to zero point six percent.
Those reductions were achieved without any changes to her antiepileptic medications.
Mila had been on a stable regimen of anti-seizure drugs throughout the pre-treatment period, and that regimen was not modified during the milasen treatment.
So the reduction in seizures could not be attributed to a change in her anti-epileptic management.
The most parsimonious explanation was that milasen itself was reducing her seizure activity.
I want to dwell on why this matters, because if you are not used to reading clinical neurology literature, the size of these effects may not register the way it should.
A sixty-three percent reduction in daily seizure count is, in clinical terms, a large, clinically meaningful effect.

For most antiepileptic drugs, the bar to declare a drug "effective" in a clinical trial is a fifty percent responder rate — meaning, the proportion of patients whose seizure count drops by at least fifty percent compared to baseline.
Achieving an actual sixty-three percent reduction in a patient with this severity of disease is, by the standards of the seizure literature, a strong response.
The eighty-five percent reduction in time spent in seizure activity — which captures both the count reduction and the duration reduction together — is even more striking.
These are also numbers that are relatively hard to fake.
A parent diary alone could conceivably be subject to bias, especially given the family's investment in the treatment working.
But the diary numbers were corroborated by EEG, which is an objective electrophysiological measurement that does not depend on observer interpretation in the same way.
When the diary and the EEG agree, the signal is real.
The seizure result was, in the paper, the most clinically compelling piece of evidence that milasen was doing something.

The other measures of Mila's clinical status — adaptive behavior scores on the Vineland-II scale, gross motor function on the GMFM-88 scale, developmental status on the Bayley scale, sensory thresholds — were more mixed in their findings.
They mostly stabilized, in the sense that her previously declining trajectory on those measures flattened during the treatment period.
She was not getting worse on those scales as fast as she had been before.

But "not getting worse as fast" is a harder thing to interpret than a clear quantitative drop in seizure frequency, because the natural history of a neurodegenerative disease is variable and the period of observation was limited.
The seizures were the clearest signal.
The other measures were consistent with stabilization but not, individually, statistically conclusive in a single patient.
Together, the clinical picture during the first year of treatment was: seizures substantially better, other functional measures plateaued rather than continuing to decline.
That is a hopeful picture.
It is also, by itself, not a cure.
And the next segment is where we have to confront, honestly, what the drug did not do.
